summary: Jeff Atwood demonstrates the alarming speed of rainbow table attacks, showing how Ophcrack can crack seemingly strong passwords like 'Fgpyyih804423' in under 3 minutes. Rainbow tables are massive pre-computed hash lookups that trade storage space for cracking speed, making brute-force attacks orders of magnitude faster. Windows systems are especially vulnerable due to legacy LAN Manager hashes that remain enabled by default. The defense against rainbow tables is straightforward: use password salting to make pre-computed tables useless, and use passphrases long enough to exceed table coverage.
key_points: Rainbow tables enable password cracking that's orders of magnitude faster than brute-force by pre-computing hash values for all character combinations
A 388MB rainbow table can cover 99.9% of all 14-character alphanumeric passwords and crack them in minutes
Windows Server 2003's default support for legacy LAN Manager hashes creates serious security vulnerabilities for domain credentials
Passphrases are immune to rainbow table attacks due to their length exceeding table coverage
Salting password hashes with a unique prefix completely defeats rainbow table attacks by making pre-computed tables worthless
money_quotes: It can crack the password 'Fgpyyih804423' in 160 seconds.
Passwords are never stored in plaintext. At least they shouldn't be, unless you're building the world's most insecure system using the world's most naïve programmers.
It's a classic time-memory tradeoff, exactly the sort of cheating shortcut you'd expect a black hat attacker to take.
This attack covered 99.9% of all possible 14 character alphanumeric passwords in 11 minutes.
There's a reason rainbow table attacks have only emerged recently, as the price of 2 to 4 gigabytes of memory in a desktop machine have approached realistic levels.
When a remote hacker obtains a large list of hashed passwords from a server or database, we're in trouble.
If you've salted your password hashes, an attacker can't use a rainbow table attack against you.
key_insight: Rainbow tables turn password cracking from a computationally expensive brute-force problem into a fast lookup operation, but salting your hashes renders these massive pre-computed tables completely useless.
